realityCheck: Houston is one of the most car-dependent big metros in the country — sidewalks are sparse and jobs are scattered across dozens of districts. Transportation is often the real deciding factor, so plan for it before you accept anything.
ifYouHaveACar: Judge jobs by commute time, not distance — traffic can turn 15 miles into a 45-minute drive.
Put gas, tolls, parking, and upkeep into your pay math. A job paying $2/hr more but adding an hour of driving and $15/day parking can net less.

ifTransportationIsLimited.detail: A fully remote support role removes the commute entirely. This is usually the strongest move when you don't have reliable transportation — use the remote search strings in the Search Toolkit.

Local bus/rail fare is $1.25 (60 cents discounted). Focus on jobs near the Red, Green, or Purple lines — Downtown, Midtown, Museum District, and especially the Texas Medical Center, which has a large number of healthcare help-desk jobs right on the Red Line. Near the University of Houston, ask about the COAST program, which can put up to $27.50/month toward your METRO fare.

METRO runs 25+ suburban Park & Ride lots with express buses to Downtown and the TMC. Boarding fare is $2-$4.50 (about $4-$9 round trip); a day pass is $3. Good if you can drive to a lot but want to skip downtown parking and traffic.

METRO Vanpool matches you with nearby commuters heading to the same area; average cost runs about $7-$9/day per rider (covers your share of the lease, gas, parking, and tolls), and some employers subsidize it further. Sign up and use the commute-cost calculator at vanpool.ridemetro.org.

metroLift: MetroLift is METRO's paratransit service for riders who can't use regular bus or rail because of a disability — worth checking eligibility if that applies to you.

resourcesIfNoCar.name: METRO Vanpool
resourcesIfNoCar.detail: Averages about $7-$9/day per rider (covers your share of the lease, gas, parking, and tolls); sign up and get matched with nearby commuters. Some employers subsidize part or all of the cost pretax.

resourcesIfNoCar.name: Rideshare to a transit stop
resourcesIfNoCar.detail: Pairing a short Uber/Lyft or carpool ride with light rail or Park & Ride is often cheaper than a rideshare for the whole commute.

resourcesIfNoCar.name: 211 Texas / United Way HelpLine
resourcesIfNoCar.detail: Dial 211 or search '211 Texas transportation assistance' for local nonprofit help with bus passes, gas cards, or rides to work.

resourcesIfNoCar.name: Employer transit benefits
resourcesIfNoCar.detail: Ask HR whether a pretax transit or parking benefit is offered; it can offset Park & Ride or rideshare costs.

resourcesIfNoCar.name: Outside Houston
resourcesIfNoCar.detail: Search '[your city] vanpool', '[your city] transit authority', or '211 [your state] transportation assistance' for the equivalent local programs.

questionsToAskBeforeAccepting: Is it onsite, hybrid, or fully remote? How many days on site?
What are the shift hours — and does transit actually run then? (Bus and rail service thins out at night and on weekends.)
Is there a parking cost, or is parking provided?
Any mileage reimbursement or transit benefit?
